refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote equipment control depends on reliable communication. The refined FR quantifies the availability target, specifies bidirectionality (command uplink + telemetry downlink), and anchors measurement to an agreed evaluation period. This is essential for safety-critical teleoperation where link loss can lead to loss of control.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state (up/down) during evaluation period; calculate availability as (total_time_link_up) / (total_evaluation_time). Acceptance: measured availability ≥ 0.999. Requires instrumentation of link health across all network segments.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall provide redundant communication paths to achieve 0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions for availability audit.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The vehicle shall maintain safe state during link loss (failsafe behavior).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Single primary network link with passive monitoring; rely on ISP SLA.
DP-B: Dual independent network links (primary + backup) with active failover logic; automatic switchover on primary link loss.
DP-C: Mesh network topology with multi-path routing and software-defined failover; dynamic link selection based on real-time quality metrics.
DP-D: Hybrid approach: dual links (primary + LTE backup) with active health monitoring, automatic failover, and graceful degradation to reduced-capability mode.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid dual-link architecture (primary fixed network + LTE backup) with active health monitoring, automatic failover logic, and graceful degradation to reduced-capability teleoperation mode during link impairment.

potential_sources: DP-D couples link availability (network infrastructure) with vehicle failsafe behavior (FR-NGHE-010.4); this is acceptable because both are necessary for safe teleoperation.
DP-D couples teleoperation system design with network operator SLA; mitigation: contractual guarantees and backup LTE coverage.
DP-D does not couple unrelated functional domains; link availability is orthogonal to vehicle propulsion, sensing, and payload control.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is minimal and justified. DP-D is decoupled from specific network technologies (allows primary link to be fiber, microwave, or fixed wireless) and from vehicle-specific control algorithms.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: LTE coverage is available at all operational sites; Risk: Rural/remote sites may lack LTE; Mitigation: Pre-site survey, alternative backup (satellite, mesh radio).

Assumption: Dual-link failover can be implemented with <100ms switchover time; Risk: Switchover latency may exceed operator tolerance; Mitigation: Extensive testing, operator training on degraded-mode behavior.

Assumption: Network operators can provide SLA guarantees on primary link; Risk: SLA may not cover all failure modes (e.g., fiber cuts); Mitigation: Contractual escalation clauses, redundant fiber routes.

Assumption: Vehicle can safely enter failsafe state during link loss; Risk: Failsafe state may not be operationally acceptable (e.g., equipment left in unsafe position); Mitigation: Operator training, site safety procedures.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) primary link technology and SLA target, (2) backup link technology and coverage guarantee, (3) failover detection threshold (e.g., packet loss >X% for >Y seconds), (4) graceful degradation modes (e.g., reduced command rate, telemetry-only, manual failsafe), (5) operator notification and override procedures.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the dual-link communication architecture, link health monitoring, failover logic, and graceful degradation modes. Interfaces with Vehicle for failsafe commands and with Safety Services for link-loss alerts. Depends on external network operators for primary and backup link provisioning.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from remote operator console; transmitted over primary or backup link.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, link quality metrics) to operator console; sourced from Vehicle module.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals link loss, link degradation, or failover events to Safety Services for operator notification and safety-critical response.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication with external network infrastructure (primary ISP link + LTE backup); Teleoperation module manages link selection, health monitoring, and failover.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic. Failover logic, health monitoring, and graceful degradation are core teleoperation concerns. Minor diffusion: Vehicle module must implement failsafe behavior (owned by Vehicle), and Safety Services must handle alerts (owned by Safety Services).

Vehicle could own link monitoring, but this would scatter network-level concerns across vehicle firmware. Vehicle should own only failsafe behavior (e.g., stop motors, lock brakes) triggered by link-loss signal from Teleoperation.

Safety Services should own safety-critical response to link loss (e.g., operator alerts, emergency procedures), not the link availability mechanism itself. Assigning link monitoring to Safety Services would conflate network engineering with safety management.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would leave implementation ambiguous. Teleoperation is the appropriate sub-module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns the dual-link architecture, failover logic, and link health monitoring. Vehicle module owns failsafe behavior (triggered by link-loss signal). Safety Services owns operator notification and safety-critical response. This allocation minimizes diffusion and aligns with architectural separation of concerns: network/communication (Teleoperation), vehicle control (Vehicle), safety management (Safety Services).
containment_risks: Risk: Failover logic may require real-time performance guarantees not met by Teleoperation module's network stack; Mitigation: Implement failover in dedicated hardware (e.g., network appliance) or real-time OS kernel, not application layer.

Risk: Link health monitoring may require access to low-level network diagnostics (e.g., packet loss, latency) not exposed by standard network APIs; Mitigation: Use network monitoring tools (e.g., SNMP, NetFlow) or custom instrumentation.

Risk: Graceful degradation modes may require coordination with Vehicle control algorithms (e.g., reduced command rate); Mitigation: Define clear interface contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle for degraded-mode operation.

Risk: LTE backup link may introduce latency or jitter incompatible with real-time teleoperation; Mitigation: Characterize LTE performance in target environments, test operator tolerance for degraded link quality.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to specify: (1) Teleoperation module owns link selection, failover detection, and graceful degradation logic; (2) Vehicle module owns failsafe behavior and responds to link-loss signal via dedicated SafetyAlert interface; (3) Safety Services owns operator notification and emergency procedures; (4) Define clear interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) with latency, jitter, and availability requirements; (5) Identify hardware/OS layer responsible for real-time failover (may be separate from Teleoperation application module).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid dual-link with failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve 0.999 link availability). The DP specifies a concrete mechanism (redundancy + active failover) to meet the quantified availability target. DP-D is implementable and testable. No logical gaps between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Failover logic diffusion: Failover detection and link selection logic must be centralized in Teleoperation module; risk of scattering logic across Vehicle and Safety Services if not carefully architected.
Failsafe behavior diffusion: Vehicle module must implement failsafe behavior (e.g., stop motors) triggered by link-loss signal; this is appropriate separation, but requires clear interface contract.

Network infrastructure diffusion: DP-D depends on external network operators (primary ISP + LTE provider); NGHE solution cannot fully control link availability; mitigation: contractual SLAs and backup coverage.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the appropriate owner of DP-D. Failover logic, link health monitoring, and graceful degradation are core teleoperation concerns and should not be scattered across Vehicle or Safety Services. Vehicle and Safety Services have clear, limited responsibilities (failsafe behavior and safety response, respectively). Containment score 0.95 for Teleoperation is justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-010 is verifiable: continuous link monitoring during evaluation period, calculation of availability ratio, comparison to 0.999 threshold. Teleoperation module can instrument link state transitions and provide audit logs. Verification requires access to network infrastructure metrics (primary link uptime, LTE coverage, failover events).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns link availability mechanism (DP-D). Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) are allocated to Teleoperation (link detection, redundancy, logging) and Vehicle (failsafe behavior). Safety Services owns safety response. No ambiguity in ownership or responsibility.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify primary link technology, backup link technology, failover detection threshold, and graceful degradation modes.
Define interface contracts (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) with explicit latency, jitter, availability, and bandwidth requirements.
Identify hardware/OS layer responsible for real-time failover (may be separate from Teleoperation application module).
Establish contractual SLAs with network operators for primary and backup links; define escalation procedures for SLA violations.
Plan for pre-site network survey to verify LTE coverage and primary link availability at operational sites.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs addressing link detection, redundancy, logging, and failsafe behavior. DP-D is sound and well-justified, but implementation requires detailed design of failover logic, link health monitoring, and graceful degradation modes. Teleoperation is the correct IM owner, but containment risks (real-time failover, network diagnostics, graceful degradation coordination) require further refinement at the next decomposition level. Proceed to decompose sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) to address these risks.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss within [X] milliseconds (e.g., 500ms) based on packet loss or timeout.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall provide redundant communication paths (primary + LTE backup) with automatic failover to achieve 0.999 availability.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall log link state transitions (up/down, failover events, link quality metrics) for availability audit and troubleshooting.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The vehicle shall enter safe failsafe state (stop motors, lock brakes, hold position) upon link loss signal from teleoperation system.

assumptions: LTE coverage is available at all operational sites; pre-site survey required.
Dual-link failover can be implemented with <100ms switchover time; extensive testing required.
Network operators can provide SLA guarantees on primary link; contractual agreements required.
Vehicle can safely enter failsafe state during link loss; operator training and site safety procedures required.
Operator can tolerate graceful degradation modes (reduced command rate, telemetry-only); operator acceptance testing required.
open_risks: Rural/remote sites may lack LTE coverage; alternative backup (satellite, mesh radio) may be required.
Switchover latency may exceed operator tolerance; extensive testing and operator training needed.
Network SLA may not cover all failure modes (e.g., fiber cuts); contractual escalation clauses and redundant routes needed.
Failsafe state may not be operationally acceptable (e.g., equipment left in unsafe position); site safety procedures and operator training needed.
Real-time failover logic may require hardware/OS layer implementation not available in standard Teleoperation application module.
Network diagnostics (packet loss, latency, jitter) may not be accessible via standard APIs; custom instrumentation may be required.
evidence_needed: Network coverage maps (primary ISP + LTE) for all operational sites.
SLA agreements with network operators specifying availability, latency, jitter, and failure response.
Failover logic design and real-time performance analysis (e.g., worst-case latency, jitter).
Graceful degradation mode specifications (e.g., reduced command rate, telemetry-only, manual failsafe).
Operator acceptance testing results for degraded-mode teleoperation.
Link availability measurement methodology and audit procedures.
Vehicle failsafe behavior specifications and testing results.
Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) for link loss scenarios and mitigation strategies.
